articles.title: AI Deepfake Nude Crisis in Schools Is Far Worse Than Anyone Knew
articles.summary: A joint investigation by WIRED and Indicator has found that nearly 90 schools and around 600 students across the world have been affected by AI-generated deepfake nude images. The scale of the problem is far larger than previously reported, and experts say it shows no signs of slowing down. The findings paint a deeply alarming picture of how easily available AI tools are being weaponised against young people, mostly girls.
articles.content:
This is not a fringe problem anymore. A new investigation by WIRED and Indicator found that close to 90 schools and roughly 600 students around the world have been caught up in incidents involving AI-generated deepfake nude images. Those are just the cases they could document. The real number is almost certainly much higher.

The way it works is straightforward and terrifying. Someone takes a real photo of a student, usually from social media, and runs it through a publicly available AI tool that strips the clothing from the image and generates a fake nude. The result looks convincing enough to cause serious harm to the person in the image, even though no real nudity was ever involved.

The victims are overwhelmingly girls. In most of the cases WIRED and Indicator tracked, the images were created by classmates and then shared within school networks. Some victims found out when the images were circulated in group chats. Others only found out when a teacher or parent saw them. In nearly every case, the emotional damage was immediate and severe.

What makes this so hard to fix is that the tools used to create these images are freely available online. No special skills are required. And while some countries have started criminalising the creation and sharing of deepfake sexual images, enforcement is patchy and schools are largely left to deal with the fallout on their own.

The WIRED and Indicator investigation is a wake-up call for parents, schools, and governments. The technology is not going away. What needs to happen urgently is clearer law, faster platform responses when images are reported, and real support for the students who are already living with the consequences of this right now.
keyPoints: Nearly 90 schools and around 600 students worldwide have been affected by AI deepfake nude images
The images are created using freely available AI tools that require no special skills
Victims are overwhelmingly girls, with classmates identified as the main perpetrators
Many victims only found out when images were already circulating in group chats
Laws criminalising deepfake sexual images exist in some countries but enforcement remains inconsistent

articles.title: Aegon's Conquest Game Of Thrones Film Is Officially Happening
articles.summary: Warner Bros. has confirmed that Aegon's Conquest, a Game of Thrones spin-off film, is in the works and part of its 2027 and beyond release slate. This is the first time a Game of Thrones story is moving from TV to the big screen, expanding an already massive franchise into new territory. For fans who have been waiting for more Westeros content, this is the news they did not know they needed.
articles.content:
Game of Thrones ended its original run in 2019 after eight seasons on HBO, and the response to that finale was famously divisive. Since then, Warner Bros. and HBO have been rebuilding goodwill with the prequel series House of the Dragon, which focuses on the Targaryen civil war known as the Dance of the Dragons. Now the franchise is making an even bigger move.

Aegon's Conquest has been officially confirmed as a Game of Thrones spin-off film by Warner Bros., as reported by NME on April 15, 2026. The film is slotted into the studio's 2027 and beyond release slate, meaning fans should start getting comfortable with the idea of Westeros on a cinema screen rather than a TV screen. Aegon's Conquest refers to the story of Aegon the First, the Targaryen king who united the Seven Kingdoms of Westeros through dragonfire and conquest, which is literally the foundational origin story of everything that happens in both Game of Thrones and House of the Dragon.

This is a smart move for Warner Bros. The Aegon story is massive in scale, full of dragon battles, political intrigue, and legendary figures. It is the kind of story that practically demands a cinematic budget and a big screen. It also gives the franchise a chance to pull in audiences who might not have kept up with House of the Dragon on HBO but would show up for a blockbuster event film.

The confirmation also signals that Warner Bros. sees Game of Thrones as a long-term cinematic universe play, not just a TV property. That is a significant shift in how the studio is treating one of its most valuable IP assets. Aegon's Conquest landing in the 2027 and beyond window puts it in conversation with some of the biggest franchise films in development right now.
keyPoints: Aegon's Conquest is officially confirmed as a Game of Thrones spin-off film by Warner Bros.
It is part of the Warner Bros. 2027 and beyond release slate
The film covers Aegon the First's conquest of Westeros, the origin story of the entire franchise
This is the first Game of Thrones story to move from TV to a feature film format
The move suggests Warner Bros. is building Game of Thrones into a full cinematic universe
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Aegon's Conquest Game of Thrones film 2027

articles.title: AI Slop Is Making Up To 35 Percent Of The Internet Fake-Happy
articles.summary: A new study examined the rise of AI-generated websites and found that up to 35 percent of the internet may now be made up of AI-generated content, much of it projecting an unnervingly cheerful and positive tone. The research shows that AI slop, the low-effort mass-produced AI content flooding the web, is not just polluting information quality but is actively warping the emotional texture of the internet. It turns out that when algorithms write the web, they default to a kind of hollow happiness that feels deeply off.
articles.content:
You have probably noticed it. You land on a website and something feels wrong. The writing is smooth but oddly upbeat. Every sentence is enthusiastic about nothing in particular. There are no rough edges, no personality, no actual human behind the words. Welcome to AI slop, and according to a new study covered by Wired on April 15, 2026, it now makes up as much as 35 percent of the internet.

The study looked at the explosion of AI-generated websites and content farms that have been quietly colonising the web. What researchers found was not just that this content exists in enormous quantities. It is that AI-generated content has a distinctive emotional signature. It skews positive. It is relentlessly, artificially upbeat in a way that human-written content simply is not. The internet, which has always been a chaotic mix of anger, joy, irony, and weirdness, is getting smoothed out into something that feels like a customer service chatbot wrote everything.

This matters for a few reasons. First, when 35 percent of what you read online is generated by AI systems optimised to sound helpful and pleasant, your sense of what the internet actually thinks starts to get distorted. You might come away feeling like the world is more agreeable than it is, or that every topic has a tidy positive spin. That is a subtle but real kind of misinformation. It is not lying about facts. It is lying about the emotional reality of human experience.

There is also a trust problem building here. People are getting better at sensing AI slop even when they cannot specifically identify it. That vague wrongness feeling is becoming more common, and it is eroding trust in web content broadly. Not just the obviously fake stuff. All of it. When the internet starts to feel fake, people start to disengage or turn to closed communities where they trust the humans behind the content.

For creators, journalists, and anyone who makes real things for the internet, this is actually a moment of opportunity buried inside a mess. Authentic human voices are becoming more valuable precisely because they are getting harder to find. The web is filling up with AI-generated noise, which means genuine signal stands out more than ever. The challenge is getting people to find it when the algorithm-optimised slop is everywhere.
keyPoints: A new study found that up to 35 percent of the internet may now be AI-generated content
AI-generated websites tend to have an artificially positive and upbeat emotional tone
This fake happiness is subtly distorting how people perceive online sentiment and opinion
Growing awareness of AI slop is eroding trust in web content more broadly
Human-made authentic content is becoming more valuable as AI noise floods the internet

articles.title: Godzilla Minus Zero Teaser Has Godzilla Destroying the Statue of Liberty
articles.summary: GKIDS and Toho have dropped the first teaser for Godzilla Minus Zero, the sequel to the smash hit Godzilla Minus One, and it shows Godzilla marching on the Statue of Liberty. The film is set for a theatrical release in November 2026. Godzilla Minus One won the Academy Award for Best Visual Effects in 2024, making it the first non-English language film to win that award, which is exactly why the stakes for this sequel feel so enormous.
articles.content:
Godzilla is going to New York, and the teaser for Godzilla Minus Zero makes it look absolutely terrifying in the best way possible.

GKIDS and Toho released the first footage from Godzilla Minus Zero this week, and the standout image is Godzilla looming over the Statue of Liberty. It is a classic monster movie power move, and the fact that it is coming from the team behind Godzilla Minus One means people are paying very close attention. Godzilla Minus Zero is hitting theaters in November 2026.

A quick backstory for anyone who needs it: Godzilla Minus One came out in late 2023 and was a genuine phenomenon. It was a Japanese film set in post World War Two Japan, and it told a raw, emotional story about trauma and survival alongside all the monster carnage you expect. It then won the Academy Award for Best Visual Effects at the 2024 Oscars, which was a massive deal because no non-English language film had ever won that award before. It was not just good for a Godzilla movie. It was just genuinely good.

So the sequel has an enormous amount to live up to. Bringing Godzilla to an American landmark like the Statue of Liberty feels like a deliberate escalation, both in scale and in symbolic weight. The Statue of Liberty is one of the most recognisable images in the world, and seeing Godzilla next to it in what appears to be a Japanese production rather than a Hollywood one carries a very specific kind of charge.

GKIDS has been the key distributor bringing Japanese animated and live action films to Western audiences for years, so their involvement is a strong signal that the goal is to make Godzilla Minus Zero a proper global theatrical event, not just a niche release for existing fans. November is a prime awards season window, which suggests Toho and GKIDS believe this film can compete again at the highest level.
keyPoints: Godzilla Minus Zero is the sequel to Godzilla Minus One and arrives in theaters November 2026
The teaser shows Godzilla attacking the Statue of Liberty
GKIDS and Toho are co-releasing the film
Godzilla Minus One won the Academy Award for Best Visual Effects in 2024, the first non-English language film to do so
A November release puts Godzilla Minus Zero squarely in awards season territory

articles.title: AI Is Quietly Making Your Brain Weaker, Study Finds
articles.summary: A new study warns that relying on AI tools has a so called boiling frog effect on human cognition, where your abilities quietly decline while your immediate results improve. The research found that AI assistance boosts short term performance but comes at a heavy cognitive cost over time. It is the kind of finding that should make anyone who uses ChatGPT daily stop and think.
articles.content:
You know the boiling frog idea. Drop a frog in hot water and it jumps out. Put it in cool water and slowly heat it up and it never notices until it is too late. A new study published in April 2026 argues that this is exactly what AI is doing to human thinking. We get better results today and do not notice our own brains getting weaker in the process.

The researchers found that AI assistance genuinely improves your immediate performance on tasks. So in the short term you are getting better outputs, faster answers, and cleaner work. The problem is what is happening underneath. As the study puts it directly, AI assistance improves immediate performance but it comes at a heavy cognitive cost. The effort, the struggle, the mental heavy lifting that actually builds skill over time is being skipped. And skipping it repeatedly has consequences.

This matters beyond just individual productivity. We are talking about how an entire generation is learning to think, write, reason, and problem solve. If the tool is always there to fill in the gap, the gap never closes on its own. Skills that used to develop through repeated effort may simply stop developing. The study frames this not as a sudden crisis but as a slow drift, hence the boiling frog comparison. By the time the effects are obvious, the damage is already done.

It is worth being clear about what this study is not saying. It is not saying AI is evil or that you should delete your apps today. AI tools are genuinely useful and can make hard things more accessible. But there is a real difference between using AI as a scaffold while you learn versus using it as a permanent replacement for thinking. The study is essentially asking which one most people are actually doing.

The timing of this research lands in an interesting moment. AI tools like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini are now embedded in schools, offices, and everyday life in ways that felt hypothetical just two years ago. The conversation about what we lose by leaning on them has lagged behind the conversation about what we gain. This study is a nudge to take the costs as seriously as the benefits.

The honest takeaway here is not panic. It is intentionality. If you are using AI to get things done faster, that is fine. But if you are using it to avoid the hard parts of thinking entirely, you may be paying a price you just cannot see yet. The frog, famously, does not notice the water getting hotter.
keyPoints: A new study found AI use has a boiling frog effect on human cognition
AI improves your results in the short term but weakens your thinking over time
The cognitive cost comes from skipping the mental effort that builds skills
The concern is not one big crisis but a slow drift that goes unnoticed
The research calls for people to be more intentional about how they use AI tools

articles.title: Nicole Kidman Is Training to Be a Death Doula
articles.summary: Nicole Kidman has revealed she is training to become a death doula, a person who provides emotional and practical support to people who are dying and to their families. Kidman says the death of her mother, Janelle Kidman, in 2024 inspired her to pursue this new direction in her life. It is a deeply personal move from one of the world's most recognisable actors, and it says a lot about how grief can completely change your priorities.
articles.content:
Nicole Kidman is not slowing down. She is just taking things in a completely different direction. The Oscar-winning Australian actor has opened up about training to become a death doula, and the reason behind it is genuinely moving.

For anyone unfamiliar, a death doula is someone who supports dying people and their loved ones through the end of life process. Think of it like a birth doula but for the other end of the journey. They provide emotional comfort, help families navigate what is happening, and make sure the person who is dying feels less alone. It is not a medical role, it is a human one.

Kidman told The Guardian that losing her mother Janelle Kidman in 2024 was the turning point. She said she wants to "provide solace and care" to people going through something similar. That is not celebrity fluff. That is someone who went through real grief and came out the other side wanting to do something about it.

Nicole Kidman has spent decades in the spotlight, from her early films in Australia to her Academy Award for The Hours in 2003 and her more recent work on shows like Nine Perfect Strangers, which dealt with trauma and alternative healing. So this move is not entirely out of left field for someone who has consistently been drawn to stories about the human mind and emotional pain.

What makes this story worth paying attention to is the bigger cultural moment it sits inside. There is a growing conversation right now about death, grief, and how badly Western culture avoids both. Death doulas are becoming more mainstream, and the fact that someone with Kidman's profile is embracing this publicly could genuinely help shift how people think about dying and the support available around it.

It is also just a reminder that celebrities are people too. Nicole Kidman lost her mum and it changed her. That is something most of us can understand, no matter how different her life looks from the outside.
keyPoints: Nicole Kidman is training to become a death doula following the 2024 death of her mother Janelle Kidman
A death doula provides emotional and practical support to dying people and their families
Kidman says she wants to provide solace and care to people going through end of life experiences
The move reflects a growing cultural conversation about death, grief, and end of life support
Kidman has long been drawn to projects exploring trauma and emotional healing

articles.title: Gen Z Is Rejecting AI and Tech Giants Should Panic
articles.summary: A new wave of data shows Gen Z is turning its back on AI in a way that no previous generation has done with emerging technology. This is a serious red flag for the entire AI industry because young people are usually the ones who drive tech adoption forward. If the generation that grew up with smartphones and TikTok is skeptical of AI, something deeper is going on.
articles.content:
Here is the thing about every major tech wave of the past few decades. Young people led the charge. Teens and twenty-somethings were the first to get on social media, the first to stream music, the first to live on their phones. The industry counted on that pattern continuing. With AI, it is not happening.

Gen Z's skepticism toward AI tools is not just casual disinterest. According to reporting from Futurism published April 15, 2026, the distrust runs deep and it is spreading. Young people are not just ignoring AI. They are actively pushing back against it. That is a completely different energy from what the tech industry expected when it started rolling out AI chatbots, AI art generators, and AI-assisted everything.

So why does Gen Z feel this way? A few things are stacking up. There is a real anxiety around AI replacing jobs, and Gen Z is the generation about to enter or just entering the workforce. There is also a growing cultural rejection of content that feels fake or soulless, and AI-generated output has become a symbol of exactly that. On top of that, Gen Z grew up watching social media hollow out attention spans and mental health. They are not eager to hand another technology that kind of power over their lives.

For the AI industry, this is not a small PR problem. It is a generational pipeline problem. If young people are not buying in now, adoption habits could calcify in ways that hurt AI companies for years. The tech world has been betting that AI would follow the same curve as the smartphone. Gen Z is making it very clear that bet might be wrong.

The deeper cultural shift here is that skepticism has become its own kind of cool for Gen Z. Rejecting AI is a statement. It signals authenticity, humanity, and a pushback against corporate tech culture. That is a hard thing for any company to market against. And it means the AI industry does not just have a product problem. It has a values problem.
keyPoints: Gen Z is unusually resistant to AI adoption compared to how young people embraced past technologies
Job replacement anxiety is a major driver of Gen Z's AI skepticism
Young people associate AI-generated content with inauthenticity and soullessness
Gen Z has watched social media damage wellbeing and is wary of repeating that with AI
If this skepticism hardens, it could hurt AI adoption rates for years to come

articles.title: Kanye West's France Concert Postponed Amid Government Pressure
articles.summary: Kanye West has postponed his planned June concert in Marseille, France, after the French government began weighing whether to ban it outright. The mayor of Marseille had publicly said he did not want the show to become a showcase for those who promote hatred. It is the latest clash between Kanye West and European authorities over his antisemitic statements and public behaviour in recent years.
articles.content:
Kanye West was supposed to perform in Marseille, France, in June 2026. That is not happening now. The show has been postponed after the French government started seriously discussing whether to ban it altogether.

The mayor of Marseille did not mince words. He said he did not want Marseille to be a showcase for those who promote hatred. That is a pretty clear signal that Kanye West was not welcome, and it looks like the pressure worked before any official ban was even put in place.

For anyone who needs a quick catch-up: Kanye West, who legally changed his name to Ye, has made a string of openly antisemitic remarks since 2022. He lost major brand deals including his partnership with Adidas over those comments, and he has faced growing protests and bans at venues around the world ever since. France has a legal framework that allows the government to block public events that are considered a threat to public order or that promote discrimination.

The postponement raises a bigger question about where Kanye West can actually perform going forward. Europe has been increasingly hostile to his tour plans, and this Marseille situation shows that even getting close to a confirmed date is not enough protection. Whether the show gets rescheduled somewhere else or quietly disappears remains to be seen.
keyPoints: Kanye West's June 2026 Marseille concert has been postponed
The French government was actively considering banning the show
The mayor of Marseille said he did not want the city used to promote hatred
Kanye West has faced widespread backlash over antisemitic statements since 2022
France has legal tools to block public events that promote discrimination

articles.title: Tom Cruise Stole CinemaCon and He Was Not Even the Main Event
articles.summary: Tom Cruise showed up at CinemaCon 2026 to preview his new Warner Bros. film and walked away as the undisputed highlight of the whole event. Cruise plays a character called Digger, described as an aging blowhard oil man, in a film that wowed the crowd even in early footage form. Warner Bros. also brought first looks at The Great Beyond, Practical Magic 2, and Dune: Part Three, while Neon previewed the Cannes sci-fi epic Hope.
articles.content:
CinemaCon is the annual event where Hollywood studios show their upcoming films to cinema owners and press in Las Vegas. Think of it as a trade show for movies, where the goal is to get exhibitors excited enough to book your films into their theatres. Studios bring trailers, footage, and sometimes the actual stars. This year, Warner Bros. brought Tom Cruise, and it turned out that was all they really needed.

Cruise is starring in a new film where he plays a character named Digger, described by IndieWire as an aging blowhard oil man. That description might sound like a departure for someone best known as Ethan Hunt in the Mission: Impossible franchise, but the early footage apparently had the room. IndieWire called Cruise the highlight of Warner Bros.' entire CinemaCon presentation, which is no small thing given the other heavy hitters on the slate.

Warner Bros. also showed early looks at The Great Beyond and Practical Magic 2. Practical Magic 2 is a sequel to the 1998 film Practical Magic, which starred Sandra Bullock and Nicole Kidman as witch sisters navigating love and family. A follow up to that film has been in the works for a while and the CinemaCon footage was a first real public glimpse. Dune: Part Three also got a showing, continuing Denis Villeneuve's adaptation of Frank Herbert's science fiction saga after the massive success of Dune: Part Two in 2024.

Earlier in the day at CinemaCon, the distributor Neon showed up with a first look at Hope, a sci-fi epic that is heading to the Cannes Film Festival. Neon is the distributor behind recent awards darlings like Longlegs and Anora, so when Neon shows up with a sci-fi film bound for Cannes, people pay attention. Details on Hope are still thin but the room apparently responded well.

The bigger story here is what CinemaCon 2026 says about where Hollywood's ambitions sit right now. Sequels and returning franchises dominate the slate. Practical Magic 2, Dune: Part Three, and Cruise in a new big-canvas studio film all point to an industry leaning into known quantities as it tries to rebuild theatrical momentum post-pandemic. But the presence of something like Hope suggests there is still appetite for swings on original material.

And then there is Cruise. At an age where most action stars have long since moved into character work or producer credits, he keeps showing up and stealing rooms. Playing Digger, an aging oil man who sounds like he could be a little ridiculous, might actually be the most interesting choice he has made in years. If the full film lands the way the CinemaCon footage suggested, 2026 could be a big year for him all over again.
keyPoints: Tom Cruise previewed his new film at CinemaCon 2026 playing a character named Digger
Digger is described as an aging blowhard oil man in a Warner Bros. film
Warner Bros. also showed early footage of The Great Beyond, Practical Magic 2, and Dune: Part Three
Neon previewed Hope, a sci-fi epic heading to the Cannes Film Festival
Cruise was called the highlight of the entire Warner Bros. CinemaCon presentation

articles.title: X's Bot Purge Is Killing Years of Curated Porn Feeds
articles.summary: X's sweeping crackdown on automated bot accounts has wiped out thousands of carefully curated adult content feeds that real users spent years building in secret. Many people kept separate private X accounts purely to follow niche adult content, and those accounts got caught in the crossfire. The collateral damage is real: years of follows, lists, and saved content gone overnight with no warning.
articles.content:
X launched a massive bot purge recently, and on paper it sounds like a good thing. Fewer fake accounts, less spam, cleaner platform. But there is a messy side effect nobody really talked about when the crackdown was announced.

A huge number of real people used X specifically to build private, highly personal adult content feeds. These were not bots. They were actual humans who spent years quietly following creators, curating lists, and keeping a separate account just for that purpose. When X swept through and nuked automated accounts, a lot of those personal accounts got flagged and removed too. The result is that people logged in one day and found years of careful curation just gone.

The reason this happened is that some of these secret accounts looked bot-like to X's detection systems. They did not post much. They mostly just followed and scrolled. That kind of passive behaviour apparently tripped the same wires as an actual bot account. X's systems are not great at telling the difference between a lurker and a spam machine.

This is a genuinely weird cultural moment. It is a reminder that a lot of people use X for adult content in ways the platform has always quietly tolerated but never really built proper tools around. There is no great backup system, no way to export your follow list, and no clear appeals process for accounts that got caught up in this. People are frustrated, and honestly, that frustration makes sense.
keyPoints: X's bot crackdown accidentally removed real users' private adult content accounts
Affected users lost years of curated follows and lists with no warning
Passive accounts that mostly just followed got flagged as bots by X's detection systems
X has no clear appeals process or way to recover lost follow lists
The incident highlights how many people rely on X specifically for adult content

articles.title: J. Cole's Pro Basketball Career Lasted Exactly One Game
articles.summary: J. Cole has ended his professional basketball stint with the CBA's Nanjing Monkey Kings after playing just one game. Cole signed with the Chinese Basketball Association team earlier this year but a lengthy work visa process cut his run short before it could really begin. It is a wild footnote in an already fascinating career, and yes, it actually happened.
articles.content:
J. Cole, one of the biggest names in rap right now, tried his hand at professional basketball in China. He signed a deal with the Nanjing Monkey Kings of the Chinese Basketball Association earlier in 2026 and genuinely suited up to play. The CBA is China's top professional basketball league, the equivalent of the NBA but based across Chinese cities. Cole actually got on the court and played one official game before things came to a halt.

The reason for his exit is not drama or injury. It is paperwork. Cole cited an unexpectedly long work visa process as the thing that ended his stint. Getting the right documentation to work as a professional athlete in China takes time, and the timeline did not line up with the Nanjing Monkey Kings' season schedule. So after one game, Cole packed up and headed home.

This is not the first time Cole has blurred the line between rap and basketball. He has been open for years about his love for the sport and his belief that he could compete at a high level. He previously had a stint with Rwanda's Patriots Basketball Club in the Basketball Africa League back in 2023, which also turned a lot of heads. The man is serious about hoops, not just as a hobby but as something he genuinely pursues.

Whether Cole laces up professionally again somewhere else remains to be seen. But one game for the Nanjing Monkey Kings is now officially part of his biography. As sports careers go, it is brief. As cultural moments go, it is very J. Cole.
keyPoints: J. Cole signed with the CBA's Nanjing Monkey Kings in early 2026
He played just one official professional game before leaving
A slow work visa process was the reason for his early exit
Cole previously played professional basketball in Africa in 2023
His basketball ambitions are a long running and very real part of his life

articles.title: Pokémon Card Drop at Costco Ends in Violence and Police
articles.summary: A Pokémon card release at Costco stores turned chaotic when early morning crowds became violent and police were called to intervene. The incident is the latest in a pattern of Pokémon card releases triggering frenzied and sometimes dangerous behaviour from collectors and resellers. It is a sign of just how intense demand for Pokémon trading cards remains in 2026.
articles.content:
Pokémon cards and Costco might seem like an unlikely combination, but Costco has become one of the go-to retailers for Pokémon card drops in recent years because of its bulk packaging and relatively low prices. When word gets out that a new release is coming, the crowds show up fast and in large numbers.

This week that went badly wrong. Shoppers gathering for an early morning Pokémon card release at Costco got caught up in violent confrontations. Police were called in to deal with the situation. The specifics of exactly what sparked the violence have not been fully detailed, but the fact that it escalated to requiring police intervention says a lot about how high the stakes feel to the people involved.

For context, Pokémon trading cards have been a flashpoint for chaotic retail scenes since a massive collector and reseller boom took hold around 2020 and 2021. Cards that cost a few dollars in a pack can resell for hundreds or even thousands on the secondary market depending on the set and the card. That resale value is what drives people to line up before stores open and, in some cases, to get physical when supplies run out.

This is a trend that shows no sign of cooling off. Retailers including Costco need to think hard about how they manage these releases before someone gets seriously hurt. A trading card should not be a reason to call the police.
keyPoints: A Pokémon card release at Costco became violent and required police intervention
Early morning crowds gathered for the drop before things turned chaotic
Pokémon card releases have triggered dangerous retail scenes since the 2020 to 2021 collector boom
Rare Pokémon cards can resell for hundreds or thousands of dollars, fuelling intense demand
Retailers need better crowd management plans for high-demand card releases
